Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to hydelaw@pacbell.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BRYAN MARKET VS. ZAK VOGLER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 20, 2015, Line 5, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. GRANTED. Moving papers shift the burden by presenting evidence the City and County was unaware that "K-rails" were placed at the intersection at the time of the crash. Plaintiff fails to present competent evidence to show actual or constructive notice, or that the "K-rails" were placed by a CCSF employee; its evidence only establishes a genuine dispute of material fact as to whether "K-rails" were at the intersection, not why they were there or whether CCSF knew they were there. This is a required element of a Government Code section 835 claim, and accordingly summary judgment is appropriate. Moreover, Plaintiff also fails to present evidence creating a genuine dispute of material fact as to whether the presence of "K-rails" at the intersection was a substantially dangerous condition even with the exercise of due care, also a required element of a Government Code section 835 claim. Here, the injury was caused by a negligent and illegal right turn, despite extensive posting prohibiting such turns. Even if the "K-rails" did block a driver's vision of a bicyclist in the bicycle lane, a driver exercising due care would never have occasion to turn across the bicycle lane in the first place. Summary judgment is also granted as to Zolger's cross-complaint for indemnity against CCSF. Zolger did not oppose the motion, and there is no basis for indemnity except Government Code section 835. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: LINDSEY E. SCULLY VS. MENLO CAPITAL GROUP, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Menlo Defendants Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 20, 2015, Line 7, PLAINTIFF LINDSEY SCULLY'S Motion To Compel Menlo Defendants Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions. Plaintiff's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Form Interrogatory 17.1 is DENIED, and sanctions as for all Parties is DENIED. Menlo Defendants responded to Form Interrogatory 17.1 via mail on May 14, 2015. Forty-five days runs on June 28, 2015, a Sunday, making responses due the following Monday, June 29, 2015. And 5 days are added for mail service. This puts any notice of motion to be served on July 6, 2015, the very day the Instant Motion was, in fact, filed and served. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ERICA INGHAM VS. THE ZAPPIA LAW FIRM, A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 20, 2015, Line 10, DEFENDANT THE ZAPPIA LAW FIRM, EDWARD ZAPPIA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. The Demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. Cal. Bus Prof. Code §§ 6204(c) states: "[T]he trial after arbitration shall be initiated by the commencement of an action in the court having jurisdiction over the amount of money in controversy within 30 days after service of notice of the award." Defendant has properly filed suit in Los Angeles County. On August 4, 2015, the Los Angeles County Superior Court held: "Cal. Bus Prof. Code §§ 6203 and 6204 and Judicial Council Form ADR Rules 104 and 105 expressly establish that jurisdiction of this post non-binding arbitration attorney fee dispute which occurred in Los Angeles County remains in Los Angeles County." Here, the causes of action alleged in the FAC arise from the fee dispute between the parties. The Los Angeles County Superior Court has ruled that it is has jurisdiction over this matter, therefore Plaintiff must bring this complaint in Los Angeles County. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The motion to compel is granted. The Court agrees with the defendant certain document request categories which seek "any and all" documents in a Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act action is overbroad and potentially covers millions of documents. The Court intends to modify, when needed, each specific document request category to focus on allegations of the subject vehicle's alleged transmission problem. The Court finds Donlen v. Ford Motor Company, 217 Cal. App. 4th 138 (2013) and Doppes v. Bentley Motors, Inc., 174 Cal. App 4th 967 (2009) support the broad scope of discoverable documentary evidence requested. The parties are directed to "meet and confer" prior to the hearing to attempt to reach agreement on reformulated and focused document request language. The Court agrees with defendant a Protective Order/Confidentiality Order is appropriate for many of the documents to be produced. The Court will hear argument as to the necessity of a "highly confidential" document designation in the context of what is understood to be the mature technology of the subject transmission. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DANIEL CRUZ-PEDROZA ET AL VS. DIGNITY HEALTH, A CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion & Notice Of Hearing To Change The Place Of Trial
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 20, 2015, Line 11, DEFENDANT DIGNITY HEALTH'S Motion To Change The Place Of Trial. DENIED. It is undisputed that Defendant Dignity Health's principal place of business is in San Francisco, establishing a statutory basis for venue. A change of venue on the ground that the change will be more convenient for witnesses is in the sound discretion of the trial court. (Churchill v. White (1953) 119 Cal.App.2d 503, 506.) Tort claims against a corporation may be tried in any county in which the liability arises as well as in the county where the corporation's principal place of business is located. (Cal. Code Civ. Pro. § 395.5.) Defendant submits one declaration to support its motion, providing the names, occupations, and residence of seven witnesses involved with Plaintiff's medical treatment. Defendant's declaration, however does not state with sufficient specificity why the witnesses will be inconvenienced. Under the circumstances of this case, the convenience of witnesses for Defendant does not support an order for change of venue. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: BAYHILLS HOLDINGS, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED VS. TP PHAM, LLC., A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings By Defendant Ph Pham
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 20, 2015, Line 12, DEFENDANT TP PHAM, LLC'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings By Defendant Ph Pham. Defendant's Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings is GRANTED. "California law, like federal law, favors enforcement of valid arbitration agreements . . . ." (Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 97.) Bayhills' claims fall within the scope of the arbitration provision, which covers "any dispute or claim . . . arising out of this Agreement or any resulting transaction." (Pham Decl. at Ex. A, 34(B).) The arbitration provision is enforceable under the FAA because Bayhills presents no argument to revoke the contract. (9 U.S.C. § 2.) The arbitration provision is also enforceable under the CAA. Because Bayhill requested an entry of default judgment against Nguyen, all of the parties to Bayhill's action are parties to the arbitration agreement and there is no risk of inconsistent rulings if the claims between Bayhills and TP Pham are arbitrated. (Code Civ. Proc., § 1281.2, subd.(c).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)
